title: Topic 219: The Psychology of Side Quests: Distraction as Design
content:
### Abstract

This paper explores the psychological underpinnings of the side quest in modern video games. It argues that side quests are not mere distractions, but rather a sophisticated design element that taps into fundamental aspects of human psychology. Drawing on research from game studies, psychology, and behavioral economics, this paper examines the motivational forces that make side quests so compelling, from the allure of autonomy and the satisfaction of competence to the irresistible pull of an unfinished task. Through a series of real-world examples and a healthy dose of humor, this paper will demonstrate that the humble side quest is a powerful tool for understanding not only what makes a good game, but also what makes us, as players and consumers, tick.

### Introduction

The fate of the world hangs in the balance. A cataclysmic evil threatens to consume all of existence, and I, the chosen one, am the only one who can stop it. But first, I have to find a frying pan for a ghost. This, in a nutshell, is the modern video game experience. We are constantly presented with epic, world-altering stakes, only to be immediately distracted by a myriad of seemingly trivial tasks. The main quest, the very reason for our virtual existence, is often relegated to the back burner while we spend hours collecting rare herbs for an alchemist, hunting down a lost chicken for a distraught farmer, or, in one particularly memorable instance, helping a sentient, depressed robot find a new purpose in life. It is a phenomenon as baffling as it is ubiquitous: the irresistible allure of the side quest.

This paper will argue that side quests are far more than mere distractions or filler content designed to pad out a game's runtime. They are, in fact, a sophisticated and powerful tool of game design, one that taps into the very core of human psychology. By examining the psychological principles that make these optional adventures so compelling, we can gain a deeper understanding not only of what makes a good game, but also of what makes us, as players and consumers, tick. From the tantalizing pull of an unfinished task to the satisfying dopamine hit of a reward well-earned, side quests offer a microcosm of the motivational forces that shape our lives. So, before you set off to save the world, take a moment to consider the humble side quest. You might just find that the path to enlightenment is paved with lost chickens and haunted frying pans.

### Literature Review

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not yet dedicated a specific field of study to the psychology of the side quest. This is a lamentable oversight, one that this paper will heroically attempt to rectify. However, we are not entirely without a compass in this uncharted territory. The fields of game studies, psychology, and behavioral economics offer a wealth of research that can be, with a little bit of intellectual elbow grease and a healthy dose of humor, applied to our noble cause. The most prominent of these is Self-Determination Theory (SDT), a macro theory of human motivation that posits that we are all driven by three innate psychological needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness [1]. In the context of video games, SDT provides a powerful framework for understanding why we so willingly abandon the main quest in favor of a seemingly endless series of optional tasks.

Autonomy, in the world of SDT, refers to our need to feel in control of our own actions and decisions. Side quests, by their very nature, are a direct appeal to this need. They are optional, a choice we make freely, and in a world where we are often bound by the rigid narrative of the main quest, this illusion of choice can be intoxicating. Competence, the second pillar of SDT, is our need to feel effective and capable. Side quests are often designed to provide a steady stream of achievable goals, each one a small victory that boosts our sense of competence and keeps us coming back for more. Finally, relatedness, our need to feel connected to others, is often fostered through the narratives and characters we encounter in side quests. We are not just collecting ten bear pelts; we are helping a struggling villager provide for his family, and in doing so, we form a bond, however fleeting, with the digital inhabitants of the game world.

Of course, no discussion of motivation would be complete without a nod to the classic distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation. Intrinsic motivation, the desire to do something for its own sake, is the holy grail of game design. It is the feeling of pure, unadulterated joy that comes from mastering a new skill or exploring a beautiful virtual world. Extrinsic motivation, on the other hand, is the desire to do something for an external reward, be it a shiny new sword, a pile of gold, or a coveted achievement. Side quests are a masterclass in the art of balancing these two types of motivation. They offer the extrinsic allure of loot and experience points, while also providing the intrinsic satisfaction of a story well told and a challenge overcome. It is a delicate dance, and one that the best games perform with a grace and elegance that would make a prima ballerina weep with envy.

### Analysis

Now that we have established a theoretical framework for our investigation, it is time to delve deeper into the psychological nitty-gritty of the side quest. What are the specific cognitive and emotional mechanisms that make these optional adventures so irresistible? The answer, it turns out, is a complex and fascinating cocktail of psychological principles, each one a potent ingredient in the intoxicating brew of the side quest.

One of the most powerful of these is the **Zeigarnik effect**, a psychological phenomenon that describes our tendency to remember uncompleted or interrupted tasks better than completed ones [2]. This is the reason why that unfinished side quest, that one last collectible you just can't seem to find, will gnaw at the back of your mind, a persistent itch that you just have to scratch. Game designers are masters of exploiting the Zeigarnik effect, littering their worlds with a seemingly endless array of open loops and unresolved plot threads, each one a siren song calling us back to the game.

Then there is the **paradox of choice**, a concept popularized by psychologist Barry Schwartz, which suggests that while we may believe that more choice is always better, an overabundance of options can actually lead to anxiety, indecision, and dissatisfaction [3]. Open-world games, with their sprawling maps and endless lists of side quests, are a prime example of the paradox of choice in action. The sheer number of things to do can be overwhelming, leading to a kind of decision paralysis. And yet, we keep coming back for more. Why? Because side quests, in their bite-sized, easily digestible format, offer a way to navigate this overwhelming world. They provide a sense of structure and purpose, a clear path forward in a world that can often feel directionless.

Finally, we cannot overlook the role of **rewards and narrative** in driving our engagement with side quests. The promise of a tangible reward, be it a powerful new weapon or a rare piece of armor, is a powerful motivator. But it is the narrative, the story that a side quest tells, that often has the most lasting impact. A well-written side quest can be a miniature masterpiece of storytelling, a self-contained narrative that enriches the game world and our connection to it. It is in these moments, when we are not just completing a task but participating in a story, that the side quest transcends its status as a mere distraction and becomes a truly meaningful experience.

### Real-World Examples

To truly appreciate the art of the side quest, we must turn to the games themselves. The world of video games is replete with examples of side quests that are so compelling, so masterfully designed, that they have become the stuff of legend. Take, for example, the “Bloody Baron” questline in *The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt*. What begins as a seemingly straightforward task—finding the Baron’s missing wife and daughter—quickly spirals into a dark and complex tale of abuse, alcoholism, and ancient curses. It is a story that is as emotionally resonant as it is morally ambiguous, and it is a testament to the power of the side quest to tell stories that are every bit as compelling as the main narrative.

Or consider the countless side quests of *The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim*. From the labyrinthine dungeons of the Dark Brotherhood to the political intrigue of the Thieves Guild, Skyrim is a world that is overflowing with optional adventures. And while many of these quests may boil down to the familiar formula of “go here, kill this, bring back that,” they are so expertly woven into the fabric of the game world that they never feel like a chore. Each quest is an opportunity to explore a new corner of the world, to meet a new character, to uncover a new piece of lore. It is this sense of discovery, this feeling that there is always something new and exciting just over the horizon, that makes the side quests of Skyrim so endlessly compelling.

And let us not forget the wonderfully weird and wacky world of the *Fallout* series. From helping a colony of ghouls launch a rocket into space to participating in a live-action superhero role-playing game, the side quests of Fallout are a masterclass in absurdist humor and creative game design. They are a reminder that not all side quests need to be epic, world-altering adventures. Sometimes, the most memorable quests are the ones that are just plain weird.

### Discussion

The psychology of the side quest has implications that extend far beyond the world of video games. In our increasingly gamified world, the principles of game design are being applied to everything from education to workplace productivity. By understanding what makes side quests so compelling, we can learn to design more engaging and effective experiences in all aspects of our lives. The same principles that keep us glued to our screens, hunting down that last collectible or completing that one last quest, can be used to motivate us to learn a new skill, to exercise more, or to be more productive at work.

But there is a dark side to this as well. The same psychological principles that can be used to create engaging and rewarding experiences can also be used to create manipulative and addictive ones. The line between a well-designed game and a Skinner box can be a fine one, and it is a line that we must be careful not to cross. The goal should be to create experiences that are not just engaging, but also meaningful and enriching. We should strive to create games that respect the player’s time and intelligence, that offer not just a fleeting distraction but a lasting sense of accomplishment and a deeper understanding of ourselves and the world around us.

### Conclusion

In the grand tapestry of the video game experience, the side quest is a vibrant and essential thread. It is a testament to the power of choice, the allure of the unfinished task, and the enduring appeal of a story well told. It is a mirror that reflects our own desires, our own motivations, our own beautifully flawed human nature. So the next time you find yourself on the verge of saving the world, only to be distracted by a lost chicken or a haunted frying pan, do not despair. Embrace the side quest. For in the end, it is not just about the destination, but about the wonderfully weird and wacky journey we take to get there.
references:
[1] Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being. *American Psychologist, 55*(1), 68–78.
[2] Zeigarnik, B. (1927). Das Behalten erledigter und unerledigter Handlungen [On the retention of completed and uncompleted tasks]. *Psychologische Forschung, 9*, 1-85.
[3] Schwartz, B. (2004). *The paradox of choice: Why more is less*. Ecco.
